sections.title: Without a cat follower or in the inventory

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Good day.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: I have no interest in speaking with you. You should bring your cat to me again. The two of us have much to discuss.

sections.title: With a cat follower or in the inventory

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Ah, how interesting... a cat. Come here to me, kitty.

lines.speaker: Cat
lines.text: Meow.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Do you think that you'll be able to communicate with me at a deeper level because you're wearing that amulet of catspeak?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Er, yes?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: How very foolish.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: So, what do you need human?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: What do you need human?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Can you tell me about the gods of the Menaphites?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: If you wish to know about the lesser gods, you should speak with Jex at the Temple of the Lesser Gods. I suppose I could spare a moment or two to tell you about the main four though.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about Tumeken.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Tumeken is the god of light and husband to Elidinis. He is the leading god of the Menaphite Pantheon.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: People don't seem to talk about him much. I'd have thought being the leading god would make him more popular.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: A common saying about Tumeken is that you see him wherever you look, but never actually notice him.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Okay... And what kind of god is he? The desert is a harsh place, so does that make him harsh as well?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: In some ways. You humans sometimes say that 'you have to be cruel to be kind'. That seems a fitting way to describe Tumeken.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So he's harsh, but for good reasons?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Indeed.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about Elidinis.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Elidinis is the goddess of fertility. The River Elid is named in her honour, for it brings life to the whole region.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: There are some who even believe the river to be Elidinis herself.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: That's an interesting take. I guess being a bringer of life makes her quite popular?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: More so than many others in the pantheon, but not as much as she once was.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Why not?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Humans are fickle. Perhaps they grew bored of her?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: That sounds unlikely.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Well if you're after the human take on things, you'd be better off asking a human.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about Icthlarin.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Icthlarin is the god of the dead. He is the son of Tumeken and Elidinis, and brother to the Devourer. As this is the city of the dead, he's quite popular here.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So are you a follower of Icthlarin?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Nothing of the sort. I have no interest in the gods or their followers. Instead, Icthlarin and I have an understanding.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Cats play a significant role when it comes to guarding the dead from the Devourer. As such, they are great allies to Icthlarin.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: As a thank you for looking after them, Icthlarin grants me eternal life.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about the Devourer.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: I'd have thought you'd be more than familiar with her by now.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I'm interested in hearing your take.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Very well. The Devourer is the goddess of destruction. As such, she is commonly considered an evil goddess, but this is an oversimplification.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How so?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Not everything that destroys is evil. Destruction is often a necessity.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Isn't that also an oversimplification? Amascut is doing far more than just destroying. She's a murderer!

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Is causing death enough to make one evil? Icthlarin is the god of the dead. By your argument, surely he is the most evil of them all.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Icthlarin doesn't actually kill people though. He just looks after those that have died.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: You clearly know little of Icthlarin and his past. Are you aware of the Mahjarrat? They once served Icthlarin, before they left him for Zamorak.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Or was it the Empty Lord they left him for... I forget.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Regardless, they are a race that have regularly associated themselves with gods considered evil. Interesting company for one such as Icthlarin to keep.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How does that make Amascut good though?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: I never said it did. I'm just saying that things are often not as straightforward as they seem, especially when it comes to Icthlarin and the Devourer.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: If you say so...

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Let's talk about something else.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Very well. What do you want to dicuss?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Nothing. I should go.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Do you have a spare amulet of catspeak?

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Well, I suppose I do. Seeing as you're such a good guardian of cats, I'll give you one. Be careful with it, though.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Paw-don me, I have something to show mew.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: Human, you make a mockery of my kin.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Come on, don't be such a sour puss.

lines.speaker: Sphinx
lines.text: You should leave, before I deliver retribution for your actions.
